2 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most developers freeze the moment an interviewer asks about their greatest weakness. The palms get sweaty, the mind goes blank, and the instinct is to reach for a safe-sounding cliché that does more harm than good.
https://youtu.be/SJtks8AQ7Jo
Here’s the part most people miss: interviewers aren’t testing for perfection. They’re testing for self-awareness. Saying “I don’t really have any weaknesses” almost always backfires, and “I’m a perfectionist” is so overused it barely registers anymore. What actually works is a structured answer built on three things — honesty, growth, and evidence.
The video above breaks down exactly how to do that using the STAR method: Situation, Task, Action, Result. You briefly describe the situation where a real weakness showed up, what was expected of you, the specific action you took to improve, and the measurable result. It’s a simple structure, but it turns a vague confession into a clear story of growth.
One example covered in the video: a developer who used to write unclear Git commit messages, to the point where a teammate lost an hour just figuring out what a commit actually changed. The fix wasn’t complicated — more descriptive commit messages and a pre-PR checklist — but the result was real: code review turnaround time dropped, and the commit history became something the whole team could actually follow.
The video also covers the most common mistakes that make a weakness sound worse than it is, like picking a “weakness” that’s actually a strength in disguise, or rambling past the point where the story stays believable. There’s also a quick challenge at the end to help you prepare your own answer before your next interview.
Watch the full video above for the complete walkthrough, and subscribe to Tech Tricks for more interview prep videos that break down exactly what interviewers are listening for.
